nativeRange.habitat: Intertidal and shallow subtidal rocky shores; also estuaries and tide pools depending on species within the genus

imageCandidates.notes: Ulva lactuca by Holger Krisp, 3000x2126. Sharp, vividly green whole ruffled sheet thallus, single subject on wet gravel — the classic Sea Lettuce look; best representative of the genus.
Ulva compressa by Rosser1954, 2582x1536. Sharp in-situ vivid green frilly mat over intertidal rocks; shows the mat-forming habit for a second Ulva species.
Ulva intestinalis by Magnefl, 3264x2448. Sharp single inflated tubular (gutweed) thallus on rock, bright green — clearly shows the diagnostic tubular form distinct from sheet-type Ulva.
Ulva linza by BioOce, 2448x3264. Sharp, vivid green tufted mat at the waterline; adds a fourth distinct Ulva species.
Ulva rigida by Dimitar Nikolov Berov, 5760x3840. In-water chartreuse-green frilly sheet on reef rock; some background water haze but frond well defined — adds a fifth distinct species.
Ulva lactuca by Tigerente, 3872x2592. Vivid green sheet thallus growing in shallow water; second lactuca angle showing in-habitat growth.
Ulva compressa by Rosser1954, 2592x1552. Sharp in-situ vivid green mat over red sandstone; second compressa angle.

summary: Sea Lettuce is a flat, mat-forming green macroalgae sold under the genus name Ulva sp. when species-level identity is not specified. It grows quickly, exports nutrients efficiently, and is palatable to most herbivorous fish and invertebrates, making it a practical refugium addition.
careNotes:
This entry covers unspecified Ulva sp. sold as Sea Lettuce without species-level identification, distinct from the species-level entries for Ulva lactuca (Clean Sea Lettuce, sw-algae-011) and Ulva intestinalis (Gutweed, sw-algae-051).

Sea Lettuce grows in a flat, mat-like pattern and is palatable to almost all herbivorous fish and invertebrates. It is commonly considered one of the easier macroalgae to keep, tolerating a range of standard reef water conditions.

For water parameters, recommended targets are temperature 22–26°C (72–78°F), pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, nitrate 5–20 ppm, and phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm. These targets are consistent with standard reef chemistry and are generally advised for Ulva-genus macroalgae in reef aquariums.

Lighting should be full-spectrum; actinic-only spectra are generally not recommended, as that narrow blue wavelength range cannot support many macroalgae species. Freshwater-planted LEDs are generally considered acceptable. Flow should be moderate — similar to a soft-coral tank — with some surface agitation, but not so strong as to tear the thallus.

As a refugium nutrient exporter, Ulva species tend to assimilate nitrate and phosphate efficiently. Regular harvesting of a portion of the clump is generally advised to export accumulated nutrients from the system. The thallus is highly palatable and is commonly used as a natural food supplement for tangs, rabbitfish, and other herbivorous reef fish.

Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and other trace elements is generally recommended for macroalgae health. Propagation in the aquarium is straightforward — detached thallus fragments will regrow under suitable conditions.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily by fragmentation in the aquarium. Ulva species also undergo sexual reproduction via isomorphic alternation of gametophyte and sporophyte generations, though deliberate management of this cycle in captivity is not documented in the hobby.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros — Sea Lettuce (Ulva sp.)
primary.notes: Sole aquarium hobby source for this genus-level Ulva sp. listing. Confirms mat-forming green macroalgae, palatable to herbivores and invertebrates. No specific water parameters on product page — supplemented by Mosaic care guide.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care: The Basics
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source. Provides canonical parameter targets: temp 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm. Also covers lighting (full-spectrum, no actinic-only), flow (soft-coral-equivalent), attachment methods, and weekly iron/iodine/trace dosing recommendation.
